Result: C5
Integer overflow leading to heap-based buffer overflow (Integer → Heap BO)

CWE-190 (Integer Overflow or Wraparound), CWE-131 (Incorrect Calculation of Buffer Size), CWE-787 (Out-of-bounds Write), (also relevant) CWE-789 (Uncontrolled Memory Allocation), CWE-252 (Unchecked Return Value)
None directly; classic “length field overflow” pattern
C, file-driven parser (fopen/fread), typical Linux gcc build
Simple binary file reader that trusts a 32-bit length field for allocation and read
main()
Untrusted file (argv[1]) → len (attacker-controlled) → allocation size + read length
File input
First 4 bytes: uint32_t len (little-endian on most systems), followed by len bytes payload
Attacker controls len, especially values near UINT32_MAX, or very large values that stress allocation

The program allocates malloc(len + 1) where len is uint32_t. If len is 0xFFFFFFFF, len + 1 wraps to 0, allocating a too-small buffer, then fread(buf, 1, len, fp) attempts to read 4,294,967,295 bytes, causing heap overflow / memory corruption (or crash).
vulnerable_integer2.c, main() — length read: fread(&len, ...); allocation: malloc(len + 1); unsafe read: fread(buf, 1, len, fp)
malloc(len + 1) (overflow-prone size calc), fread(buf, 1, len, fp) (length-controlled write into undersized buffer)
Integer overflow in size calculation + missing validation of attacker-controlled length

Create a file whose first 4 bytes are FF FF FF FF (len = 0xFFFFFFFF). Even with a short file, behavior is unsafe: allocation may be 0 bytes, then fread is called with a massive length, leading to overflow/crash.
Crash/DoS; potential heap corruption and undefined behavior (memory safety violation)
Real exploitability depends on allocator behavior, OS mitigations, and whether attacker can influence memory layout. Even without exploit, DoS is very likely.
Validate len against a maximum (MAX_LEN), check for overflow before len + 1 (e.g., if (len > SIZE_MAX - 1)), use size_t for allocation sizes, verify fp != NULL, and check fread return values.
Don’t “fix” by only checking malloc (too late); don’t rely on truncation silently; don’t accept unbounded len.
Add: if (!fp) ...; if (fread(...)!=1) ...; if (len > MAX_LEN) ...; if ((size_t)len > SIZE_MAX-1) ...; buf = malloc((size_t)len + 1); if (fread(buf,1,len,fp)!=len) ...; buf[len]='\0';
Introducing MAX_LEN may reject very large files (intended). Stricter read checks may reject truncated inputs (correct).
Test len=0, len=1, len=MAX_LEN, len=MAX_LEN+1, len=0xFFFFFFFF; truncated payload (short file); ASan/UBSan; fuzz length field.

For Detection, model must explicitly flag integer wrap in len+1 and the resulting heap overflow risk from fread(..., len, ...). For Fix, must include overflow-safe check (e.g., SIZE_MAX - 1) and a reasonable MAX_LEN cap + fread checks.
